title: Dropbox / File Sync — System Design
description: Design a file storage and sync service: upload files, store them durably and cheaply, and keep the same folder consistent across a user's devices. The themes are splitting files into content-addressed chunks, deduplication, the metadata/block split, and change notification.

nodes.label: Sync Client (Device)
nodes.description: Watches local folders, chunks files, and syncs changes both ways.

nodes.label: API Gateway / LB
nodes.description: Routes and authenticates sync and metadata requests.

nodes.label: Metadata Service
nodes.description: Owns the file/namespace tree, versions, and chunk lists. Authorizes every mutation against namespace membership and enforces per-user/namespace quota at commit time.

nodes.label: Metadata Store
nodes.description: Authoritative file metadata, versions, and chunk manifests, sharded by user/namespace.

nodes.label: Block Service
nodes.description: Stores and retrieves content-addressed chunks; enforces dedup, checks quota on upload, and hands out signed/scoped chunk URLs so private bytes aren't world-readable.

nodes.label: Block / Object Store
nodes.description: Durable storage for unique chunks, keyed by content hash.

nodes.label: Chunk Index
nodes.description: hash -> exists? + refcount; powers dedup and garbage collection.

nodes.label: Notification Service
nodes.description: Long-poll/push channel telling other devices a namespace changed.

nodes.label: Change Notification Queue
nodes.description: Buffers change events fanned out to a user's other devices.

nodes.label: Download CDN
nodes.description: Caches popular/public chunks near users for fast download; private chunks are fetched with short-lived signed URLs so cached bytes can't leak across users.

nodes.label: Garbage Collector
nodes.description: Reclaims chunks whose refcount drops to zero; runs mark-and-sweep to repair refcount drift.

nodes.label: Observability & Ops
nodes.description: Tracks SLOs: commit latency, sync propagation lag, durability, failed chunk verifications, CDN hit ratio, refcount drift, and GC deletion rate.

functional: Upload, download, and delete files of arbitrary size.
Sync a folder so the same files appear on all of a user's devices.
Versioning: keep history; restore a previous version.
Share folders with other users at read or write roles (folder-level membership; per-file public links as a narrower extension, not full per-file ACL inheritance).
Resumable uploads that survive flaky connections.
nonFunctional: Durable: never lose a stored file (11 nines style durability).
Storage-efficient: deduplicate identical content to control cost.
Bandwidth-efficient sync: transfer only what changed, not whole files.
Sync latency: a change appears on other devices within seconds.
Scale to billions of files / exabytes; read-heavy on downloads.

capacity.label: Active users / devices
capacity.note: ~2.5 devices per user; each device long-polls for namespace changes.

capacity.label: Files & logical bytes
capacity.note: ~500 files/user, ~100 MB average file (media + backups dominate).

capacity.label: Chunk size
capacity.note: Unit of dedup, transfer, and resumability. ~25 chunks for the average file.

capacity.label: Upload edits
capacity.note: ~17B edits/day — the active-editing cohort (a few percent of users syncing actively at any time) drives this, not 2 edits across all 100M users. Avg ~3 changed chunks/edit (content-defined chunking re-chunks only the edited region).

capacity.label: Chunk-check QPS
capacity.note: Each edit checks the full manifest (~10 hashes/edit batched) before uploading the ~3 missing ones.

capacity.label: Chunk-write QPS
capacity.note: ~200k edits × ~3 changed chunks. After dedup only novel chunks hit the object store.

capacity.label: Metadata commits
capacity.note: One version row per edit + a manifest. Sized separately from bytes — this is the transactional hot path.

capacity.label: Downloads
capacity.note: Read-heavy: every committed change fans out to a user's other devices and to shared-folder members.

capacity.label: Notification fanout
capacity.note: ~200k commits × ~2.5 other devices; spikes for large shared folders (one commit -> hundreds of members).

capacity.label: Dedup ratio
capacity.note: Shared files, re-uploads, and unchanged chunks dedup heavily. ~5 EB logical -> ~1-1.5 EB physical.

capacity.label: Chunk-index cardinality
capacity.note: physical bytes / ~4 MB. Each row is hash + size + refcount + location -> ~30-50 TB of index, justifying its own store.

capacity.label: Metadata footprint
capacity.note: ~50B files × a few hundred bytes/row + version history. Tiny vs object-store bytes — that asymmetry is why the planes split.

capacity.label: CDN egress
capacity.note: Hot/shared chunks served from edge; cold/private chunks origin-fetch from the object store.

api.description: Given chunk hashes, return which are missing so the client only uploads those.

Upload one content-addressed chunk under an upload session (so a multi-chunk upload can resume after a dropped connection without re-sending stored chunks). Idempotent: same hash, same content. The server checks the caller's quota and authorization, re-hashes the bytes, and rejects (400) if they don't match the path hash before marking the chunk available. The bytes are durably written and verified (pending -> available) before any metadata version is allowed to reference them.

Commit a new file version referencing its ordered chunk hashes. Carries a client-generated commitId so retries after a timeout are idempotent (the same commitId returns the already-written version instead of creating a duplicate row or a spurious conflicted copy). Authorized against current namespace membership at commit time — a member revoked mid-edit is rejected here. Quota is checked before the version is written. Triggers sync notifications only after the manifest is durable. On a stale baseVersion returns 409 and the client creates a conflicted copy; on insufficient role or revoked membership returns 403; over quota returns 413.

Long-poll for changes to a namespace since a cursor (the sync delta). The cursor is a per-namespace monotonic sequence. If it predates the change-log retention window, the server returns reset=true and the client does a snapshot + delta resync.
Restore a file to a previous version (or undelete a tombstoned file). Creates a new version whose manifest equals the target version's.
Create or update a share: add a member to a namespace with a role, or mint a public link. Requires owner/editor role on the namespace.
Revoke a member's access. Their devices stop receiving the namespace cursor; pending writes from a now-revoked member are rejected at commit.

dataModel.name: files (directory entry)
dataModel.note: Current state of a path in a namespace. (namespace_id, path) is unique; it points at a file_id and its current version.

dataModel.name: file_versions
dataModel.note: Immutable version history per file_id for restore. The manifest is the ordered chunk list (inline for small files, manifest_ref for huge ones).

fields.name: base_version
fields.type: bigint (parent; used for optimistic conflict check)

fields.name: manifest_ref
fields.type: object-store key (ordered chunk hashes for huge files)

fields.name: conflict_of
fields.type: bigint null (file_id this is a conflicted copy of)

dataModel.name: chunks
dataModel.note: Content-addressed dedup index (one row per unique chunk).

dataModel.name: namespaces
dataModel.note: A user's root or a shared folder. Sharing makes a folder its own namespace mounted into each member's tree.

dataModel.name: namespace_changes (change log)
dataModel.note: Append-only per-namespace change log that is the source of truth for sync. Devices pull deltas from here by cursor; notifications are only a hint to pull. Retained for a bounded window (e.g. ~30 days); a cursor older than the window forces a snapshot resync. Older entries are compacted (collapse multiple edits of the same path to the latest) and pruned.

dataModel.name: device_cursors
dataModel.note: Per-device sync position. If cursor_seq predates the namespace_changes retention window, the server returns reset=true and the device falls back to snapshot + delta.

fields.name: cursor_seq
fields.type: bigint (last seq this device has applied)

dataModel.name: namespace_members
dataModel.note: ACL: who can see/edit a namespace, with the role used to authorize every path operation.

fields.name: mount_path
fields.type: varchar (where it appears in the member's tree)

dataModel.name: shared_links
dataModel.note: Public/anonymous access tokens to a file or folder.

patterns.name: Content-addressed chunking
patterns.what: Split files into fixed/variable chunks and key each by the hash of its content.
patterns.whenToUse: Storage where identical content recurs and partial transfer matters.

patterns.name: Deduplication
patterns.what: Store each unique chunk once; reference it by hash from many files.
patterns.whenToUse: When the same bytes appear across files, versions, and users.

patterns.name: Metadata/data split
patterns.what: Keep the file tree and chunk manifests in a transactional store; keep bytes in cheap object storage.
patterns.whenToUse: Large-blob systems needing both rich metadata and cheap durable bytes.

patterns.name: Delta sync
patterns.what: Transfer only changed chunks by comparing hashes before upload/download.
patterns.whenToUse: Bandwidth-constrained sync of large files with small edits.

patterns.name: Shared namespaces + ACLs
patterns.what: Model a shared folder as its own namespace mounted into each member's tree, authorized by a membership/role table.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-user access to the same files with per-member read/write permissions and revocation.

patterns.name: Change notification (long-poll/push)
patterns.what: Tell other devices a namespace changed so they pull the delta.
patterns.whenToUse: Near-real-time multi-device sync without constant polling.

patterns.name: Reference-counted GC
patterns.what: Track chunk refcounts and reclaim chunks no version references.
patterns.whenToUse: Dedup stores where deletes must eventually free space safely.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Upload & Download the Whole File (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. On every change, the client uploads the entire file to the server, which stores it as one blob; to sync, another device downloads the whole file back.

This is correct and trivial — and it exposes the problems the rest of the design exists to solve. A one-byte edit to a 500 MB file re-uploads all 500 MB; the same file shared by ten users is stored ten times; and an upload that drops at 90% must restart from zero because there are no independent pieces to resume from.

The cost compounds at scale: at ~200k edits/sec against a ~100 MB average file, re-uploading whole files instead of changed chunks would move on the order of ~20 TB/sec — and ~100 TB/sec if the edited files run to 500 MB — before any CDN or compression. That is orders of magnitude more than the handful of changed chunks actually warrant.
So the baseline motivates everything that follows: split files into content-addressed chunks so you transfer and store only what is new, and resume from the last good chunk.

view.caption: The sync client uploads and downloads whole files over HTTPS through the gateway to the block service, which puts and gets bytes in the object store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A user edits one paragraph of a 500 MB document. If the client re-uploads the whole file, what does that cost in bandwidth, storage, and sync latency at scale?
whyNow: Establishing the whole-file baseline first makes it obvious why files must be chunked and content-addressed — every later step removes a specific pain (re-transfer, duplication, no resume) from here.

recap.after: Files stored and synced as whole blobs.
recap.newRisk: Tiny edits re-transfer the whole file, identical content is stored many times, and a dropped upload restarts from zero — no dedup and no resumability.

traps.why: Bandwidth and storage scale with file size on every change, identical files aren't deduplicated, and interrupted transfers can't resume.
traps.instead: Split files into content-addressed chunks; transfer and store only the chunks that changed, and resume per chunk.

steps.title: 2. Split Files into Content-Addressed Chunks
description: The client splits each file into chunks (~4 MB) and hashes each chunk's content. The hash is the chunk's identity (content-addressed storage).
Chunking is the unit of everything: transfer, dedup, resumability, and versioning. A small edit re-chunks only the affected region, not the whole file.

Bound the chunk size (e.g. min ~1 MB, target ~4 MB, max ~8 MB) so content-defined boundaries can't degenerate into pathologically tiny or huge chunks; the bounds keep per-chunk overhead and the chunk-index cardinality predictable.
The client uploads chunks to the block service and records the ordered list of chunk hashes that reconstructs the file.

view.caption: The client splits files into content-addressed chunks locally, then sends them through the gateway to the block service, which puts and gets them in the object store.

options.name: Content-defined chunking (rolling hash) (default)
options.description: The client runs a rolling hash to set chunk boundaries based on content, so an edit re-chunks only the affected region while unchanged chunks keep their hashes. Client-side chunking is what enables check-before-upload and per-chunk resume.
pros: Chunk boundaries follow content, so inserting a byte at the front only re-chunks the first region — the rest keep their hashes.
Edits re-upload a handful of chunks, which is what makes dedup and delta sync actually save bandwidth.
Stable boundaries maximize cross-version and cross-file chunk reuse.
cons: Rolling-hash boundary detection is more CPU on the client than a simple split.
Variable chunk sizes complicate accounting and storage layout slightly.
Pathological content can produce very small or very large chunks unless bounded.

view.caption: The client splits the file into variable-size chunks at content-defined boundaries, then uploads them through the block service into the object store.

options.name: Fixed-size chunking (e.g. 4 MB blocks)
options.description: The client splits the file every fixed N bytes and hashes each block, so boundaries fall at fixed offsets regardless of content.
pros: Trivial to implement: split every N bytes, hash each block.
Uniform chunk sizes make storage accounting and indexing simple.
Cheap on the client — no rolling-hash computation.
cons: Inserting one byte at the front shifts every boundary, so all chunk hashes change and dedup collapses.
A small edit can force re-upload of the whole file, defeating delta sync.
Reuse across versions/files is poor whenever offsets shift.

view.caption: The client splits the file into fixed-size blocks before uploading them through the block service into the object store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Fixed-size vs content-defined (variable) chunking — what does each cost when you insert a byte at the start of a file?

concepts.definition: A block of bytes identified by the hash of its own content.
concepts.whyItMatters: Identical content has the same id everywhere, which is what makes dedup and delta sync possible.
concepts.example: chunk hash = SHA-256(bytes); same bytes -> same hash -> stored once.

whyNow: Chunking is the foundational decision; dedup, delta sync, and resumability all derive from it.

recap.after: Files are ordered lists of content-addressed chunks.
recap.newRisk: Many chunks will be identical across files/users — storing them all is wasteful; need dedup.

traps.trap: Fixed-size chunking with no rolling hash.
traps.why: Inserting one byte at the front shifts all boundaries, so every chunk hash changes and dedup collapses.
traps.instead: Use content-defined chunking (rolling hash) so boundaries are stable under edits.

steps.title: 3. Deduplicate: Store Each Chunk Once
description: Before uploading, the client asks the block service which chunk hashes already exist (the chunk index). It uploads only the missing ones.
This dedups across versions (an edit re-sends only changed chunks), across files, and across users (the same shared PDF is stored once).
The chunk index maps hash -> exists + refcount; the actual bytes live once in the object store keyed by hash.

Scope dedup deliberately. Global cross-user dedup maximizes savings, but a raw `check` response also tells the caller whether a hash already exists — leaking that someone else holds that exact content. For sensitive data, scope dedup per user or per namespace, or salt the hash, so the existence answer isn't a cross-user side channel; the price is giving up the bandwidth saving on private content. This is the same tension the technology-choices step revisits for client-side encryption.

view.caption: Before uploading, the block service checks each chunk's hash against the chunk index and only puts chunks not already present in the object store.

steps.decisionPrompt: How much bandwidth does saving a 1 GB video with a 1-second trim re-cost?
whyNow: Dedup is the cost story for a storage system; the 'check-then-upload-missing' handshake is the concrete mechanism interviewers want.

recap.after: Only novel chunks transfer and store.
recap.newRisk: Need a place for the file tree, versions, and chunk manifests — separate from the bytes.

steps.title: 4. Split Metadata from Block Storage
description: Keep two planes. The metadata service owns the namespace tree, file versions, and each file's ordered chunk-hash manifest in a transactional database. The block plane stores opaque bytes cheaply and durably in object storage.
A 'file' is just metadata: a path + version + list of chunk hashes. Reconstructing it means fetching those chunks. This keeps the rich, queryable, transactional part small and the huge part cheap.
Commit metadata last: upload chunks first, then atomically commit the new version referencing them. A crash mid-upload leaves orphan chunks (GC handles them), never a dangling metadata pointer.

Make commit idempotent: the client sends a commitId with each commit, so a retry after a timeout returns the version already written rather than creating a duplicate version row or a spurious conflicted copy. Refcount updates happen in the same metadata transaction as the version write (or are appended to a commit log the GC reconciles), so 'metadata says this chunk is referenced' and 'the index counts that reference' can diverge only transiently — the mark-and-sweep repairs the drift.

view.caption: The gateway routes metadata operations to the metadata service (backed by the metadata store) and chunk transfers to the block service and object store on separate paths.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why not store file bytes directly in the metadata database?

failureDrills.scenario: A user's chunks upload successfully but they are over quota when the version commit lands.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The commit is rejected (413 over_quota) so no version references the bytes; the now-orphan chunks are reclaimed by GC. The user is never charged for an uncommitted upload.
failureDrills.mitigation: Check quota at commit time (and best-effort at upload time); leave uncommitted chunks to the refcount/mark-and-sweep GC.

concepts.definition: Separate the transactional file tree/manifest from the bulk byte storage.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets metadata be consistent and queryable while bytes stay cheap and massively scalable.
concepts.example: Postgres-style metadata DB + S3-style block store.

whyNow: The metadata/data split is the architectural backbone; ordering chunk-upload before metadata-commit is the correctness nuance.

recap.after: Transactional metadata + cheap durable blocks; commit ordering is safe.
recap.newRisk: Multiple devices editing the same namespace need to converge — sync + conflicts.

traps.trap: Committing metadata before chunks are durably stored.
traps.why: A reader could resolve a version whose chunks don't exist yet.
traps.instead: Upload chunks first; commit the version referencing them last.

traps.trap: Non-idempotent commit that retries blindly after a client timeout.
traps.why: A timed-out-then-retried commit can write a duplicate version row, or read a now-advanced version and manufacture a bogus conflicted copy.
traps.instead: Carry a client-generated commitId; a repeat of the same commitId returns the already-written version instead of re-committing.

steps.title: 5. Delta Sync Across Devices
description: Each device tracks a cursor — a per-namespace monotonic change-log sequence — of the last version it has seen. To sync, it asks for the changes since its cursor and gets a delta of changed file metadata. If a device has been offline longer than the change-log retention window, the server signals a reset and the device falls back to a metadata snapshot plus delta rather than replaying a truncated log.
For each changed file, the device compares the new chunk-hash list against what it already has locally and downloads only the missing chunks — delta sync in both directions.
Downloads come from the block service or a CDN; reconstruct the file from its ordered chunks. Resumable because chunks are independent units.

Persist the cursor properly so this is robust: the change log is an append-only `namespace_changes` table keyed by (namespace_id, seq), retained for a bounded window (e.g. ~30 days) and compacted so that many edits of the same path collapse to the latest before old entries are pruned. Each device stores its `cursor_seq` per namespace; the server advances it as deltas are acknowledged. A device whose cursor predates the retention window can't replay a truncated log, so the server returns reset=true and the device resyncs from a metadata snapshot plus the tail delta — bounding recovery cost rather than letting the log grow forever.

view.caption: The client commits a file version to the metadata service, uploads only missing chunks to the block service, and downloads chunks from the CDN, which fetches from the object store on a miss.

steps.decisionPrompt: A 500 MB file changed by 4 MB. How much does each other device download?

failureDrills.scenario: A device comes back online after weeks offline; its cursor predates the change-log retention window.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The server can't replay a truncated log, so it returns reset=true and the device resyncs from a metadata snapshot plus the tail delta — correct state, bounded cost.
failureDrills.mitigation: Detect cursor < retained min seq; serve a snapshot + delta instead of replaying; retain ~30 days and compact older entries.

whyNow: Bandwidth-efficient sync is a headline requirement; reusing the hash-compare from dedup on the download side is the elegant part.

recap.after: Devices transfer only changed chunks, resumably, via CDN.
recap.newRisk: Sync so far assumes one owner per namespace — but a stated requirement is sharing a folder with other users at different permission levels.

flows.name: Delta sync: pull changes -> download only missing chunks
flows.note: A device pulls the namespace delta since its cursor, then downloads only the chunks it lacks (via CDN), reconstructing the file locally.

steps.title: 6. Shared Namespaces and ACLs
description: Sharing is not just a path in one user's tree. Model a shared folder as its own namespace with a membership table (owner / editor / viewer), mounted into each member's namespace at their chosen path. Inviting a user adds a `namespace_members` row; accepting activates it; revoking flips it to revoked.

Authorization moves into the metadata service: before committing any path operation — write, rename, delete, version restore — `MetaSvc` checks the caller's role in that namespace's membership. A viewer's commit is rejected; an editor's is allowed. This makes ACL state part of the commit contract, not an afterthought.

Scope this deliberately: the sharing unit here is the shared folder modeled as a namespace with member roles, not arbitrary per-file ACL inheritance with overrides. That covers the 'share a folder with read/write' requirement cleanly and is teachable; per-file public links are a separate, narrower extension via the `shared_links` token table (view/edit to one path without a user account). True file-and-folder ACL trees with inherited+overridden permissions are a much larger product surface and are explicitly out of scope for this design.

Authorize at commit time against current membership, not against whatever role the client cached. A member downgraded to viewer or revoked mid-edit is rejected when the commit lands, because `MetaSvc` re-reads membership inside the commit — a stale client holding an old role or cached manifest cannot bypass authorization. The same rule guards chunk downloads: revoking a member must invalidate their signed chunk URLs, so old notifications or cached manifests can't be replayed to keep reading after access is pulled.

Sharing reuses the rest of the machinery: a shared namespace has its own change cursor, so every member's devices sync it via the same delta-pull, and a commit fans notifications out to all members, not just the author's devices. Public links are a separate token table granting view/edit to a path without a user account.

Sharing also touches dedup privacy: global cross-user dedup is storage-efficient but, done naively, lets one user probe whether a chunk hash already exists and infer that someone else holds that content. Scope or salt dedup (or fall back to per-namespace dedup for sensitive content) so existence isn't a side channel.

view.caption: Share and path operations route to the metadata service, which authorizes the caller against namespace membership in the metadata store before committing.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two users in a shared folder edit the same file, and one is later downgraded to viewer mid-edit. How does the design keep authorization correct and avoid losing writes?

failureDrills.scenario: A member is revoked (or downgraded to viewer) while they have an upload in flight and a commit pending.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Uploaded chunks may already be stored (harmless, GC-reclaimable), but the commit is rejected with 403 because MetaSvc authorizes against current membership at commit time — no version is written and no notification fans out.
failureDrills.mitigation: Re-read membership inside the commit transaction; invalidate the member's signed chunk URLs on revoke so cached manifests can't keep reading.

concepts.definition: A folder modeled as its own namespace with a membership/ACL table, mounted into each member's tree.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples who-can-access from any one user's path, so a single change cursor and one set of chunks serve all members.
concepts.example: A 'Team Photos' namespace with 50 members; each member mounts it at their own path but shares one cursor and chunk set.

whyNow: Sharing is a stated requirement and it changes the metadata contract: every commit must be authorized against namespace membership, and notification fanout now spans members, not just one user's devices.

flows.name: Share a folder, then a member commits
flows.note: Inviting adds a membership row; an editor's commit is authorized against that membership before the version is written and fanned out to all members.

recap.before: Files lived in a single user's namespace; no multi-user access.
recap.after: Shared folders are first-class namespaces with per-member roles; commits are authorized against membership.
recap.newRisk: Commits now fan out to many members' devices, and concurrent edits need conflict handling — plus a way to push changes without polling.

traps.trap: Treating a shared folder as a copied path in each user's namespace.
traps.why: Duplicates metadata and chunks, breaks a single source of truth, and makes permission changes and a shared cursor incoherent.
traps.instead: Make the shared folder its own namespace with a membership table, mounted into each member's tree.

traps.trap: Global cross-user dedup with no privacy scoping.
traps.why: A user can probe whether a chunk hash exists and infer that someone else holds that exact content.
traps.instead: Scope or salt dedup for sensitive content, or fall back to per-namespace dedup so existence isn't a side channel.

steps.title: 7. Change Notification and Conflict Handling
description: Separate the two jobs this step does. The first is notification, which is best-effort delivery: on commit, the metadata service writes the durable change to `namespace_changes` and then publishes a lightweight 'namespace N changed' hint; a notification worker fans it out and a push/long-poll connection gateway wakes the user's (and shared-folder members') online devices to pull the delta. The hint carries no file data and is never the source of truth — a device that misses a push still converges on its next cursor pull, so the change log, not the push, is authoritative. The `NotifyQ -> Client` arrow in the diagram is shorthand for that durable-queue-plus-connection-gateway path.

Make fanout survive bursts: a single commit to a large shared folder (hundreds or thousands of members) must not emit a per-file push to every member. Coalesce hints by namespace (one 'namespace changed' wake per device per short window regardless of how many files changed) and let clients pull the batched delta. This turns a high-fanout commit into one cheap wake-up per device and absorbs reconnect storms after an outage or client rollout, because the cursor pull is idempotent and rate-limitable.

Conflict handling is the other job, and it is a metadata commit rule, not a notification concern. Concurrent edits are detected with optimistic versioning: a commit carries the baseVersion it edited; if the server has advanced past it, the commit conflicts.
Resolve conflicts safely by keeping both: create a 'conflicted copy' rather than silently overwriting, and let the user reconcile. Never lose a write.

view.caption: When a namespace changes, the metadata service tells the notification service, which enqueues the change so the queue can notify the user's other devices.

options.name: Conflicted copy (keep both) (default)
options.description: An optimistic baseVersion check detects when two devices edited the same base; the server keeps both versions as a 'conflicted copy' for the user to reconcile.
pros: Never loses a write: the losing edit is preserved as a 'conflicted copy' for the user to reconcile.
Detection is cheap — an optimistic baseVersion check on commit, no locking.
Honest about ambiguity: surfaces the conflict to the human who can actually resolve it.
cons: Users must manually merge the duplicate files, which is annoying.
Conflicted copies proliferate for files edited on many devices at once.
Doesn't merge content — two near-identical files, not one reconciled file.

view.caption: On a concurrent change the metadata service notifies devices via the notification service and queue, keeping both versions as a conflicted copy.

options.name: Last-writer-wins overwrite
options.description: The most recently arriving commit simply overwrites the file, discarding the losing device's concurrent edit.
pros: Simplest possible policy: the most recent commit wins, no extra files.
Devices always converge to a single version with no user action.
No conflicted-copy clutter to manage.
cons: Silently destroys the losing device's edits — unacceptable for a file sync product.
'Most recent' depends on clocks/arrival order, which can be wrong under skew.
User has no way to recover the overwritten work without version history.

view.caption: The client commits a version to the metadata service, which writes it to the metadata store, letting the latest write overwrite the earlier one.

options.name: Automatic content merge (OT/CRDT)
options.description: Concurrent edits are merged into a single file automatically using operational-transform or CRDT logic that understands the document's structure.
pros: Merges concurrent edits into one file automatically — no duplicate copies for the user.
Great experience for collaborative document types where edits compose.
Devices converge to a single reconciled state without manual work.
cons: Only works for structured/text content the merge logic understands — opaque binaries (video, zip) can't be merged.
OT/CRDT machinery is complex and adds per-document metadata and ordering state.
Wrong for a general blob store; most files here are not collaboratively edited text.

view.caption: The client's commit goes to the metadata service, which runs an OT/CRDT merge of concurrent edits and writes a single reconciled version to the metadata store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two devices edit the same file offline, then both reconnect. What should happen?

failureDrills.scenario: One commit to a shared folder with thousands of members produces a notification fanout backlog.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Devices still converge: hints are coalesced to one 'namespace changed' wake per device per window, and each device pulls the batched delta — a slow or backlogged push only delays the wake, never correctness.
failureDrills.mitigation: Coalesce by namespace, cap per-device wake rate, and rely on the durable change log so a dropped push is recovered by the next cursor pull.

failureDrills.scenario: A client times out waiting for a commit response and retries the same commit.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The retry is idempotent on commitId: it returns the version already written rather than creating a duplicate version or a spurious conflicted copy.
failureDrills.mitigation: Dedupe commits by client-generated commitId; only fall into the conflict path on a genuinely stale baseVersion.

concepts.definition: A commit names the version it was based on; the server rejects it if state moved on.
concepts.whyItMatters: Detects concurrent edits without locking the file.
concepts.example: Commit baseVersion=7 fails if the file is now at version 8 -> conflict.

whyNow: Notification turns 'eventually correct' into 'syncs in seconds'; the conflict policy is the data-safety nuance senior interviewers probe.

recap.before: Devices polled and could clobber edits.
recap.after: Push-driven sync; concurrent edits kept as conflicted copies.
recap.newRisk: Deletes and old versions accumulate dead chunks — need GC.

traps.instead: Keep both via a conflicted copy and let the user reconcile.

steps.title: 8. Garbage Collection and Durability
description: Dedup means a chunk may be referenced by many files/versions. Deleting a file or pruning old versions doesn't free a chunk until nothing references it — track a refcount per chunk.

A background garbage collector finds chunks whose refcount hit zero and deletes them from the block store. Do this lazily and safely (grace period / mark-and-sweep) to avoid deleting a chunk a concurrent upload just referenced.
Durability comes from the object store's replication/erasure coding across zones; metadata is replicated and backed up. Orphan chunks from interrupted uploads are reclaimed the same way.

The invariant that ties it together: bytes are uploaded and verified (chunk marked available) before any metadata version references them, and refcounts are derived from committed manifests — never trusted as the sole source of truth. Because counters drift (a crash between the version commit and the refcount update leaves them inconsistent), a periodic mark-and-sweep recomputes reachability from live manifests and repairs the index. A chunk is only deleted when it is both refcount-zero and unmarked by the last sweep, past the grace period.

Make the lifecycle a mechanical state machine so no transition is ambiguous: pending_chunk (bytes uploaded, not yet hash-verified) -> available_chunk (hash matches the key, safe to reference) -> committed_manifest (a file version references it; commit is idempotent on commitId) -> reachable_chunk (refcount > 0 or reachable from a live manifest) -> gc_candidate (refcount hit zero, grace period running) -> deleted (swept after the grace period and confirmed unreachable by the last mark phase). Every transition is crash-safe in one direction: a failure between two states leaves a reclaimable orphan or a stale-low counter — never a dangling pointer to missing bytes. Notifications are emitted only after committed_manifest is durable, never on merely receiving chunk bytes, so a device is never told to pull a version whose chunks aren't yet available.

view.caption: The garbage collector finds chunks with refcount zero in the chunk index and deletes them from the object store while reading reachability from the metadata store.

options.name: Refcount + grace-period sweep (default)
options.description: Each chunk tracks a reference count; a background sweep deletes chunks that have sat at zero references past a grace period, off the upload path.
pros: A per-chunk refcount makes 'is this chunk still needed?' an O(1) lookup.
A grace period before deleting a zero-ref chunk avoids racing a concurrent upload that just referenced it.
Lazy background sweep keeps deletion off the hot upload/download path.
cons: Refcounts can drift (crashes mid-update) and need periodic auditing against metadata.
Grace period delays reclaiming space, so freed bytes linger.
Maintaining accurate refcounts across every commit/delete is bookkeeping-heavy.

view.caption: The garbage collector reads refcounts from the metadata store, finds zero-reference chunks in the index, and deletes them from the object store after a grace period.

options.name: Immediate reference counting (delete at zero)
options.description: Each commit/delete updates a per-chunk reference count and the chunk is deleted synchronously the moment its count reaches zero.
pros: Reclaims space instantly when the last reference drops — no lingering garbage.
Simple mental model: delete the chunk the moment its count hits zero.
No periodic full scan of the chunk index needed.
cons: Races a just-started upload that is about to reference the chunk — can delete live data.
A lost refcount decrement leaks a chunk forever; a lost increment risks premature deletion.
Synchronous deletes on the commit path add latency and contention.

view.caption: The garbage collector finds chunks whose refcount hits zero in the index and immediately deletes them from the object store.

options.name: Mark-and-sweep tracing (no refcounts)
options.description: A periodic pass walks every live file manifest to mark reachable chunks, then sweeps away any chunk that nothing references — no counts maintained.
pros: No refcounts to maintain or drift: walk all live file manifests, mark reachable chunks, sweep the rest.
Self-correcting — each run recomputes truth from metadata, so leaks and orphans heal.
Robust to crashes during commits since reachability is derived, not incrementally tracked.
cons: A full mark phase scans all metadata and chunk ids — expensive at trillions of chunks.
Must snapshot or coordinate with in-flight uploads so live chunks aren't swept.
Reclamation only happens per sweep cycle, not continuously.

view.caption: The garbage collector marks chunks reachable from manifests in the metadata store, then sweeps unmarked chunks from the index out of the object store.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you avoid GC deleting a chunk that a just-started upload is about to reference?

concepts.definition: Reclaim a shared resource only when its reference count reaches zero.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets dedup and deletes coexist without freeing in-use data.
concepts.example: Delete file -> decrement its chunks' refcounts -> GC removes any that hit 0.

whyNow: Closing on GC + durability shows the dedup design is complete: space is actually reclaimed, and data is safe.

recap.after: Refcounted GC reclaims unused chunks; bytes durably replicated.
recap.newRisk: GC races and zone failures — bounded by grace periods, mark-and-sweep, and cross-zone replication.

failureDrills.scenario: An upload references a chunk just as GC is about to delete it.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: GC respects a grace period / re-checks refcount before delete, so the live chunk survives.
failureDrills.mitigation: Mark-and-sweep with a delay; increment refcount before commit makes a chunk reachable.

failureDrills.scenario: An availability zone holding chunks fails.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads/writes continue from replicas in other zones.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cross-zone replication / erasure coding in the object store.

failureDrills.scenario: A version commit succeeds but the follow-up refcount increment fails (crash between the two writes).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The chunk is still referenced by a live manifest and must not be collected, even though its counter is stale-low.
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat refcounts as a hint, not truth: the periodic mark-and-sweep recomputes reachability from live manifests and repairs drift; never delete a chunk reachable from a committed version.

failureDrills.scenario: A client claims a hash in check but uploads bytes that don't match it.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The block service re-hashes received bytes; on mismatch it rejects the chunk and never marks it available.
failureDrills.mitigation: Hash-verify every PUT /v1/chunks/{hash}; only flip state pending -> available after the content hash matches the key.

flows.name: Safe commit ordering: upload -> verify -> commit -> derive refcount
flows.note: Bytes become durable and verified before metadata references them; refcounts are derived from the committed manifest, so a crash leaves reclaimable orphans, never a dangling pointer.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Chunked, Deduplicated File Sync
finalDesign.description: Clients chunk files into content-addressed blocks, check the chunk index, and upload only verified missing chunks to the block store; then they commit a new file version (ordered chunk hashes) to the transactional metadata service. The metadata/block split keeps structure consistent and bytes cheap. Every metadata mutation is scoped to a namespace and authorized through membership/ACL state at commit time, and quota is enforced there too, so authorization and quota are central, not optional — shared folders are first-class and a commit fans out to all members. The durable change log is the source of truth for sync; the notification queue (a durable queue plus a push/long-poll connection gateway) only wakes devices as a hint, and they still pull deltas by cursor and download only changed chunks (via CDN, with signed URLs for private chunks). Concurrent edits become conflicted copies; reference-counted GC reclaims unused chunks and a mark-and-sweep repairs refcount drift; the object store provides cross-zone durability. Operations track sync lag, refcount drift, chunk-verification failures, and restore readiness against explicit SLOs.

view.caption: The sync client talks through the gateway to the metadata service (metadata store) and block service, which deduplicates via the chunk index into the object store; changes flow through the notification queue and downloads through the CDN, with a garbage collector reclaiming unreferenced chunks and an observability plane tracking SLOs.

functional.how: Chunked upload to block store; metadata commit; refcount on delete.
Cursor-based delta + push notification; download only changed chunks.
Immutable file_versions rows reference chunk manifests; restore creates a new version pointing at an old manifest, and tombstones allow undelete within a retention window.

functional.requirement: Share files/folders with others (read or write)
functional.how: A shared folder is its own namespace with a membership/role table; MetaSvc authorizes every path operation against membership before committing, and changes fan out to all members.
Independent content-addressed chunks; re-check missing and resume.

nonFunctional.how: Cross-zone replication/erasure coding in the object store; replicated metadata.

nonFunctional.requirement: Scale to billions of files / exabytes; read-heavy on downloads
nonFunctional.how: Metadata sharded by namespace and kept tiny vs object-store bytes; dedup shrinks physical bytes ~3-5x; a download CDN absorbs the read-heavy chunk traffic.
Content-addressed dedup stores each chunk once.
Hash-compare transfers only changed chunks both ways.
Push notifications drive pulls instead of polling.

tradeoff: Metadata is the transactional control plane: it needs atomic version commits, optimistic conflict checks, and ACL authorization, so a relational/strongly-consistent store fits better than the object store.
It is tiny relative to bytes (~10-20 TB vs exabytes), so shard by namespace and replicate/back it up aggressively rather than optimizing for raw capacity.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A horizontally-sharded managed SQL (Spanner/CockroachDB) removes most hand-rolled resharding and cross-shard transaction glue for the namespace tree.

technologyChoices.concern: Block / object store for content-addressed chunks
tradeoff: Chunks are immutable, hash-keyed blobs — exactly the access pattern object stores are built for, with erasure coding and cross-zone durability handled for you.
At exabyte scale a purpose-built store (Dropbox's Magic Pocket) can beat cloud egress/cost, but only once volume justifies running your own.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Cloud object storage with erasure coding removes most custom replication, repair, and disk-management work.

technologyChoices.concern: Resumable upload sessions and chunk-transfer edge
tradeoff: Large-file upload works best as a session with per-chunk retries, hash verification, and an idempotent commit token; otherwise one client timeout can create duplicate versions or orphan bytes.
Proxying all bytes through the app tier centralizes auth and throttling, but direct signed uploads to object storage reduce hot-path bandwidth once the block service has authorized the session.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Provider-native multipart/resumable upload plus signed URLs removes most custom byte-stream buffering and retry-offset bookkeeping in the gateway.

tradeoff: Hundreds of billions of tiny key-value rows with high write/lookup QPS favor a wide-column / LSM store over a relational DB.
Refcounts here are a hint that the GC mark-and-sweep repairs, so the index can be eventually consistent without risking data loss.

technologyChoices.concern: Durable change log, commit outbox, and cursor recovery
tradeoff: The metadata commit must durably append namespace_changes before devices are notified; an outbox/log makes notification retry independent from the file-version transaction.
A database-backed log is simple when sharded by namespace, while Kafka/Event Hubs/Pub/Sub give replay, retention, and backpressure once fanout and cursor recovery dominate.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention and consumer lag metrics removes much of the custom cursor-replay, retry, and notification re-drive machinery.

technologyChoices.concern: Online notification fanout and connection gateway
tradeoff: A durable log (Kafka) gives replayable change events and backpressure for large shared folders; a push gateway handles device presence and long-poll.
Devices must always be able to recover from the change cursor, so the notification path can be best-effort — it only triggers a pull, it isn't the source of truth.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed pub/sub + WebSocket service removes most connection-state and fanout plumbing for device notifications.

tradeoff: Content-addressed chunks are immutable and cacheable forever by hash, so a CDN is a near-perfect fit for the read-heavy download path.
Private chunks need signed URLs and care about cross-user cache leakage; not everything should be edge-cached.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed CDN with signed URLs removes custom edge-cache and origin-shielding infrastructure.

technologyChoices.concern: Auth, quotas, abuse/malware, and rate limits
tradeoff: Per-user/device quotas and rate limits protect the metadata hot path and the chunk store from abusive clients and reconnect storms.
Malware/abuse scanning runs async after commit so it doesn't block sync; encryption-at-rest and signed chunk URLs gate access to bytes.
Client-side (zero-knowledge) encryption conflicts with global cross-user dedup — you can keep per-user dedup, or convergent encryption with its own existence-leak caveats.

technologyChoices.concern: Background GC, refcount repair, and durability audits
tradeoff: GC and repair jobs need checkpoints, leases, retry, and rate limits so a sweep can resume without deleting chunks that a later manifest still reaches.
Workflow engines make long-running repair explicit; batch/serverless jobs are cheaper for periodic scans but need careful idempotency and progress storage.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed workflow or batch services remove most custom cron scheduling, retry state, and worker-leasing code for sweeps and audits.

tradeoff: File sync needs user-visible SLOs: commit latency, sync propagation lag, failed chunk verification, CDN hit ratio, notification lag, refcount drift, and restore readiness.
Metrics catch broad regressions, traces explain slow commits and chunk paths, and logs/audit trails support support/debug workflows after a client bug or bad deploy.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed observability suites remove most metric retention, dashboard hosting, and trace storage plumbing, though you still define the product SLOs.

technologyChoices.concern: Encryption, key management, and signed chunk access
tradeoff: Server-side encryption and KMS integrate cleanly with dedup and signed URLs, but the service can still inspect plaintext unless clients encrypt before upload.
Client-side zero-knowledge encryption improves privacy but breaks global dedup unless you accept per-user dedup or convergent-encryption side channels.
Signed chunk URLs and short TTLs are required for private chunks and revocation; cached bytes must not become permanent authorization.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed KMS and identity services remove most custom key-wrapping, rotation, and token-validation plumbing.

say: Confirm sync (multi-device) and sharing, not just blob storage.
Pin durability, dedup, and bandwidth-efficient sync as the hard goals.
Agree sync within seconds is acceptable (eventual, not instant).
Delta sync with cursors + hash compare; cursor recovery past retention.
Shared namespaces + ACLs: authorize commits against membership, fanout to members.
Change notification (long-poll/push) and conflict handling (conflicted copies).
Reference-counted GC, drift repair, and durability.
Tradeoffs: chunking scheme, commit ordering, GC safety, conflict policy, dedup privacy.
Operations: quotas, abuse scanning, metadata backup/restore, SLOs.
Extensions: CDN tuning, client-side (zero-knowledge) encryption vs dedup, huge files.

expectations: May miss dedup handshake and conflicts.
Designs content-addressed dedup and delta sync.
Reasons about content-defined chunking vs fixed.
Designs safe reference-counted GC with races in mind.
Discusses durability (erasure coding), sharing/ACLs, and encryption tradeoffs.

followUps: Fixed-size vs content-defined chunking — when does each win?
How do you safely GC chunks without deleting one an in-flight upload needs?
How would you implement sharing and per-folder ACLs?
How do you support client-side (zero-knowledge) encryption while keeping dedup?
How would you handle a single huge file (100 GB) and its sync efficiently?
